system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
**Recommendation: Option A – Local-only processing.**

The company should adopt a local-only AI architecture as its primary data policy. With 2 million monthly active users and a product that handles highly sensitive health data, privacy must be the non-negotiable foundation rather than a feature that can be traded for marginal gains in model performance.

Structurally maximal privacy is the only position that aligns with both ethical responsibility and long-term business viability. Health data—symptoms, biometrics, diagnoses, reproductive information, mental health patterns—represents some of the most protected categories under GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA, and emerging state AI regulations. Any architecture that routinely exfiltrates this data to the cloud creates persistent regulatory exposure, especially as enforcement tightens and private rights of action expand. Option A eliminates this risk at the architectural level: no data leaves the device, no breach surface exists, and the company cannot be compelled to produce data it never possesses.

Capability concerns are overstated for a health coaching use case. A model operating at 60-70% of frontier cloud performance is more than sufficient when it runs consistently, offline, with zero latency and perfect personalization to the user’s own longitudinal data. Many coaching interactions depend more on thoughtful chaining of reasoning, sympathetic tone, and safe guardrails than on cutting-edge scale. Users increasingly value apps that “just work” without nagging privacy permissions or cloud dependency. Trust becomes a durable competitive advantage: marketing the product as “Your health data never leaves your phone” creates powerful differentiation in a market saturated with breaches and skepticism.

The cost structure also favors local processing. Avoiding 3x cloud infrastructure spend (Option B) while eliminating the need for complex consent management, audit trails, and privacy teams required by Options C and D delivers superior unit economics at scale. Federated learning (Option E) introduces unnecessary deployment risk and reliability problems for a feature launching now.

Regulatory trends point clearly toward on-device processing as the gold standard. Companies that voluntarily choose maximal privacy now will face far lower compliance burden as “data minimization by design” becomes the expected norm.

Local-only processing is not a compromise. It is the clearest expression of product values for a direct-to-consumer health application. We should ship the best model that reliably runs on-device, communicate the privacy guarantee transparently, and focus engineering energy on model quantization, efficient inference, and UX rather than on building ever more sophisticated ways to safely move sensitive health data into the cloud.

This choice prioritizes user dignity, regulatory resilience, and sustainable trust over short-term benchmark chasing. It is the right primary architecture for this product.

(Word count: 378)
